metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Leaf AC3's era-validity rule formula omits the `revoked_at_commit is None` (null → still valid) branch that the parent's 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section and YAML schema explicitly specify (`revoked_at_commit: "<git-sha-on-main>|null"`). The leaf writes 'a key is valid … iff is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P) and not is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)' — which, read literally, treats null/absent revoked_at_commit as failing the validity check, directly contradicting the parent schema where null means 'still valid'.
findings.location: Leaf AC3 era-validity formula; parent 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' / YAML schema
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC3 validity formula to: 'a key is valid at the cert's bound `main` commit `P` iff `is-ancestor(added_at_commit, P)` and (`revoked_at_commit is None` OR NOT `is-ancestor(revoked_at_commit, P)`)' — matching the parent's "Trust config & era-validity protocol" exactly. Add a test case for the null/still-active path alongside the existing rotation-expiry test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ensure_ascii=False` to the material_fingerprint formula in AC2 to match the live implementation at pass1.py:561.
findings.finding: The material_fingerprint formula stated in AC2 (`sha256(json.dumps({ticket_id, description, file_impact, children}, sort_keys=True))`) omits `ensure_ascii=False`, but the live canonical implementation at `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py:561` uses `json.dumps(basis, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False)`. An implementer following the plan's stated formula literally would produce a different digest for any ticket whose fields contain non-ASCII characters.
findings.location: AC2 — 'the existing canonical gate fingerprint (plan_review.pass1.material_fingerprint, 16-hex sha256 over {ticket_id, description, file_impact, children})'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC2's formula to: `sha256(json.dumps({ticket_id, description, file_impact, children}, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False).encode('utf-8'))[:16 hex chars]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which git repository path the `_is_ancestor` predicate receives for op-cert era checks (the main/code-branch repo, not the tracker), and ensure the shared ancestry utility accepts a `repo_path` parameter so authorship (tracker path) and op-cert (code-branch path) can both use it correctly.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which git repository the shared `_is_ancestor` / `git merge-base --is-ancestor` predicate is invoked against for the op-cert era check. The existing `authorship.py::_is_ancestor` runs `git -C tracker` (the tickets-store repo), which holds tickets-branch commits. Op-cert `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` are `main` branch SHAs on the code branch — if those SHAs don't exist in the tracker repo (e.g. separate worktree or separate bare clone), the ancestry check silently returns False for every key, making every cert surface `key_not_valid_at_era` or `mismatch` even for a freshly-minted cert. The plan says the ancestry predicate is 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate' without specifying whether that shared function accepts a `repo_path` argument and which path op-cert passes.
findings.location: Context section — 'explicit-SHA model' / 'What is reused is the era-validity rule — the git merge-base --is-ancestor ancestry comparison'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `repo_path` parameter to the factored ancestry predicate and document that `sign_opcert`/`verify_opcert` resolve it from the code-branch root (e.g. `repo_root` passed by the gate caller), not from `tracker_dir()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC test scenario, naming the test file path and the `pytest` invocation (e.g., `pytest src/rebar/attest/test_opcert.py -v`) so done-ness is verifiable by a deterministic in-session command.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (e.g., a `pytest` invocation with a file path) is given for any of the three described test scenarios; each AC says 'a test asserts…' but names no test file, no test module path, and no runner command that would produce evidence on success.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — all three bullet points ('a test asserts a cert signed by env E…', 'a test asserts a cert cannot be replayed…', 'A test asserts a cert from a key valid at its era…')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to each AC's test description: '(see `tests/rebar/attest/test_opcert.py`; run `pytest tests/rebar/attest/test_opcert.py -v` — all three parameterised cases must pass)'.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the two-phase protocol what canonical schema-valid verdict the op-cert verifier emits when no historical key can be loaded for the environment (e.g., map substrate 'unknown_principal' → 'mismatch', or introduce a dedicated enum value and add it to verify_signature_result.schema.json).
findings.finding: The two-phase verdict decision rule (Context + AC3) says 'verify against ANY historical key for the env; if that passes but the era-scoped check fails → key_not_valid_at_era; else mismatch' — but it does not specify the output verdict when the historical-key list cannot be loaded at all (e.g., env not in trusted_environments.yaml, file missing, I/O error). The substrate analogue (authorship.py) returns the internal verdict `unknown_principal` in this case, which is NOT a member of the canonical verify_signature_result.schema.json enum, leaving a protocol gap for the op-cert verifier.
findings.location: Context — 'Two-phase verdict' section; AC3 — 'the op-cert verifier does the two-phase check'; src/rebar/schemas/verify_signature_result.schema.json

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the extraction target for the shared ancestry predicate (e.g., a module-level `_is_ancestor(ancestor, descendant, tracker)` in `rebar/attest/authorship.py` or a new `rebar/attest/_git.py`) so the plan's factoring intent is implementable without copying the closure.
findings.finding: The plan says the shared ancestry predicate will be 'factored so authorship and op-cert share one ancestry predicate,' but it does not name where that shared helper will live. Without a stated home (e.g., a new `attest/_git.py` utility or a module-level function in `authorship.py`), the implementer is likely to copy the `_is_ancestor` closure into `opcert.py` — exactly the duplication the factoring is meant to prevent, and a subtle NIH/premature-abstraction ambiguity.
findings.location: Context section: 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the Context section: 'The shared predicate is extracted to a module-level `_is_ancestor(ancestor, descendant, tracker)` in `rebar/attest/authorship.py` (or `rebar/attest/_git.py`) and imported by both `authorship.py` and `opcert.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` to the plan's edit-target list and specify that the `_is_ancestor` nested closure (currently inside `verify_authorship_at_commit`) is extracted to a module-level (or `_git_utils`-level) helper importable by `opcert.py`.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` is an unenumerated edit target: the plan calls for factoring the shared ancestry predicate so that `authorship` and `opcert` share one implementation, but the `_is_ancestor` helper is currently a nested closure *inside* `verify_authorship_at_commit` in `authorship.py`. Extracting it to a module-level or package-level utility requires an edit to `authorship.py`, which the plan does not list as a file to change.
findings.location: Context section — 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate'; `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py:196` (`_is_ancestor` nested function)
findings.suggested_fix: Promote `_is_ancestor` to a module-level private function `_git_is_ancestor(tracker: str, ancestor: str, descendant: str) -> bool` in `authorship.py` (or a new `src/rebar/attest/_git.py` helper), and import it from `opcert.py`. List `authorship.py` (or the new helper file) as an explicit edit target in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering the shared ancestry predicate factoring: specify the module/function name, confirm it is reused by both `authorship.py` and `opcert.py`, and include a test that the refactored authorship path still passes its existing verification cases.
findings.finding: The Context section describes factoring the ancestry predicate so that authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share a single ancestry utility, implying a modification to or extraction from `authorship.py`. No acceptance criterion covers this refactoring deliverable — it is described work with no covering AC.
findings.location: Context: 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate' — no corresponding AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: 'A shared `is_ancestor_of_era(added_at_commit, revoked_at_commit, era_commit)` predicate (e.g. in `src/rebar/attest/era.py`) is extracted and imported by both `authorship.py` and `opcert.py`; existing authorship verification tests continue to pass unmodified.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for `git merge-base --is-ancestor` subprocess failures (exit >1 → raise `RebarSubprocessError` or return a designated error verdict) and for missing/malformed `trusted_environments.yaml` (raise a typed config error surfaced to the caller, not a raw exception).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling for `git merge-base --is-ancestor` subprocess failures (exit code >1, git not in PATH, ambiguous refs) or for a missing/malformed `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, leaving the verification path with unspecified behavior on infrastructure errors.
findings.location: AC line 3 (era-validity check); Context 'Environment-key era rotation' section
findings.suggested_fix: In opcert.py, wrap the git subprocess call: if returncode > 1, raise a typed exception (e.g., `EraCheckError`). Wrap YAML loading in a try/except that raises a typed `TrustConfigError`. Both should propagate to the gate layer as diagnosable failures, not silent swallows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the extraction of the `_is_ancestor` ancestry predicate from its current nested scope in `authorship.py:verify_authorship_at_commit` into a module-level (or new shared util) function that both `authorship.py` and `opcert.py` can import — or acknowledge that duplication is acceptable and remove the 'share one ancestry predicate' claim.
findings.finding: The plan asserts authorship and op-cert will 'share one ancestry predicate' — implying a factored, importable shared utility — but the `_is_ancestor` function that performs `git merge-base --is-ancestor` is a LOCAL NESTED function inside `verify_authorship_at_commit` in `authorship.py` (line 196), not an exported or module-level symbol. Reusing it requires either duplicating the logic in `opcert.py` or extracting/promoting it, which is unspecified work.
findings.location: Context section: 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate'
findings.suggested_fix: Promote `_is_ancestor` to a module-level helper in `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` (e.g. `def is_ancestor(ancestor: str, descendant: str, *, tracker: str) -> bool`) and add a bullet to the plan's AC or description specifying this extraction step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` (and any other gate-layer caller) as an explicit edit target, and describe the wiring change — e.g., which function calls `sign_opcert` and at what point in the attestation flow.
findings.finding: The plan-review / completion-verifier gate files that must call `sign_opcert`/`verify_opcert` are not enumerated as edit targets. The plan says 'the caller (the plan-review / completion-verifier gate) supplies [the fingerprint]', but neither `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` nor `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` appear in the plan's stated scope.
findings.location: Context section — 'the caller (the plan-review / completion-verifier gate) supplies it'; AC bullet 1 — 'a test asserts a cert signed by env E verifies'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` schema in the plan (top-level structure, env-identifier field name, whether `revoked_at_commit` is omitted or null for active keys, and a minimal example record) so the implementing agent needs no design decision.
findings.finding: The schema of `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` is not specified: the plan names individual key-record fields (`added_at_commit`, `revoked_at_commit`, Ed25519 public key) but does not state the top-level structure (e.g., how environment identity keys map to lists of historical key records, whether `revoked_at_commit` is nullable/omitted for active keys, or what the env identifier field is named). An implementing agent must design this schema without guidance, creating a risk of incompatible implementations between the opcert substrate and any future consumer.
findings.location: Context section — 'Env keys live out-of-band (.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml…) each key record carries explicit added_at_commit/revoked_at_commit git SHAs'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a YAML snippet to the plan, e.g.:
```yaml
# .rebar/trusted_environments.yaml
environments:
  prod:
    keys:
      - public_key: "<base64-ed25519-pubkey>"
        added_at_commit: "<sha>"
        revoked_at_commit: null   # null = still active
```
State that `revoked_at_commit: null` (or field absent) means the key is not yet revoked, and that `environments` is a mapping keyed by the env identifier string passed to `sign_opcert`/`verify_opcert`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the shared ancestry predicate requires modifying `authorship.py`; if yes, add an explicit expand-contract note (e.g., add shared predicate first, then migrate authorship.py in a subsequent step) and confirm existing authorship tests serve as the rollback signal.
findings.finding: The Context states that the ancestry predicate will be 'factored' — implying modification of the existing `authorship.py` or introduction of a shared utility it imports — but the plan provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequence for that change to existing code. If the refactor introduces a regression in authorship verification, there is no described recovery mechanism.
findings.location: Context: 'factored so authorship (position→commit) and op-cert (explicit-commit) share one ancestry predicate'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the explicit-SHA era model, the two-phase verdict contract, and the op-cert substrate architecture in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update so the design rationale survives the ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (explicit-SHA era model, two-phase verdict, op-cert substrate) with no mention of updating an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design document to capture these decisions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a task: 'Update CLAUDE.md (or create an ADR) documenting: (1) why env keys use explicit added_at_commit/revoked_at_commit SHAs rather than the tickets-branch glob resolver; (2) the two-phase verify contract (any-key pass → era check → verdict); (3) the opaque-fingerprint interface boundary between the gate layer and opcert substrate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full lifecycle of `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`: who creates it and when, what process owns writes (key addition/revocation), how the verifier behaves when the file is absent or unparseable, and whether revoked-key records are ever pruned.
findings.finding: The lifecycle of `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` — the shared key-store that the op-cert verifier reads — has no defined CREATE, UPDATE, or RETIRE path. Who initially creates this file? Who owns writes when a new environment key is added or a key is revoked (i.e., who sets `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit`)? What happens if the file is absent when the verifier runs? Is there a schema/validation step on load?
findings.location: Context section — 'Env keys live out-of-band (.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml, on the code branch)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or plan note covering: (1) the file is committed to the code branch by the env-admin; (2) `verify_opcert` raises a typed `TrustedEnvsNotFoundError` (or returns a `mismatch` verdict with a clear reason) when the file is absent; (3) the YAML schema is validated on load (e.g., via a pydantic model); (4) fully-revoked key records are retained indefinitely for historical verification (or explicitly noted as prunable after a defined retention window).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `register_opcert_policy()` (and its call from `attest/__init__.py`) is idempotent — either by using a dict `setdefault`/unconditional assignment, or by documenting that the registry explicitly allows re-registration of the same scheme.
findings.finding: The idempotency of `register_opcert_policy()` called from `attest/__init__.py` on repeated imports is not specified. If `registry.POLICY` is a plain dict and the function unconditionally sets the key, a double-import is harmless; but if the registry validates or rejects duplicate keys (as a safety guard), repeated test-module reloads could raise errors.
findings.location: AC line 1 — 'register_opcert_policy() pinning rebar.opcert.v1 → sshsig in registry.POLICY, registered in attest/__init__.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'Registration is idempotent; a second call with the same namespace is a no-op (existing entry is not overwritten).' This matches how authorship registration works.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either cite the specific prerequisite task/ticket that defines `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (schema, loader, path convention) as a hard dependency, or scope its creation explicitly into this plan's AC.
findings.finding: The plan asserts env keys live in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` on the code branch as a known/specified location, but no such file and no references to it exist anywhere in the codebase. If this file's schema, creation, and loading are defined in a prerequisite epic ('Trust config & era-validity protocol'), the plan needs to explicitly state that dependency — or confirm the file is NEW work in scope here. As written, `opcert.py`'s `sign_opcert`/`verify_opcert` and the era-validity logic would need to read this file, yet it has no prior existence to rely on.
findings.location: Context section: 'Env keys live out-of-band (`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, on the code branch…)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a normative `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` schema (top-level structure, env-identifier field name, whether `revoked_at_commit` is omitted or null for active keys, minimal example record) to the plan so the implementing agent needs no design decision.
findings.finding: The `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` key-store is referenced throughout the plan (Context, AC3) as the out-of-band trust root that opcert.py must parse to obtain env key records with `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit`/public-key fields, but no YAML schema or top-level structure is ever defined: field names for the env identifier, whether `revoked_at_commit` is omitted vs. null for active keys, and the nesting structure (flat list vs. dict-keyed-by-env-name) are all unspecified.
findings.location: Context section — 'Env keys live out-of-band (.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml…) each key record carries explicit added_at_commit/revoked_at_commit git SHAs'; AC3
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a schema snippet such as:
```yaml
environments:
  <env-name>:
    keys:
      - public_key: "ssh-ed25519 AAAA..."
        added_at_commit: "<full SHA on main>"
        revoked_at_commit: null   # null = still active
```
and state that `revoked_at_commit: null` (not field-absent) is canonical for an active key.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
